g.data-narration: Welcome! Today we'll learn how to compare means of two small samples using the t-test, a crucial skill for data-driven engineering decisions.
g.text: Test of Significance for Means of Two Small Samples

g.data-narration: When comparing two groups, large samples allow Z-tests. But in real engineering, data is expensive. Small samples under 30 require the t-test for reliability.
g.text: Use Z-test based on normal distribution

g.data-narration: The t-distribution has heavier tails than normal distribution, accounting for uncertainty in small samples. Its shape depends on degrees of freedom.
g.text: Similar to normal but with heavier tails
Degrees of freedom: df = n₁ + n₂ - 2

g.data-narration: For the two-sample t-test to be valid, three key assumptions must be met: normality, independence, and equal population variances.
We formulate null hypothesis H₀ stating no difference between means, versus alternative H₁. Tests can be one-tailed or two-tailed.
The pooled variance combines both sample variances weighted by their degrees of freedom, assuming equal population variances.
The t-statistic measures how many standard errors the difference between sample means falls from zero under the null hypothesis.
Let's compare two sorting algorithms. We have execution times from 10 runs each. Algorithm A is the standard, B is the new design.
Plugging in our values: pooled variance equals (9×15.2 + 9×12.8) divided by 18, giving us 14.0. The pooled standard deviation is √14 ≈ 3.74.
The t-statistic equals difference in means (3.0) divided by standard error (1.67), giving approximately 1.80. This is our calculated t-value.
For one-tailed test at α=0.05 with df=18, the critical t-value is 1.734. Since 1.80 > 1.734, we reject the null hypothesis.
We conclude with 95% confidence that Algorithm B has significantly faster execution time than Algorithm A. The difference is statistically meaningful.
Remember: Use t-test for small independent samples, assume equal variances, pool the variance, compare calculated t to critical t for decision making.

g.text: Use t-test when n < 30 (population variance unknown)
Three assumptions: normality, independence, equal variance
Pooled variance combines both sample variances

g.data-narration: This test is essential in tech for A/B testing, comparing algorithm performance, analyzing optimization impact, and validating research findings in computer science.
Statistical significance does not guarantee practical significance. Always consider the context and real-world impact of your findings beyond the p-value.
g.text: Statistical significance ≠ Practical significance. Always consider the magnitude of difference
and real-world context when drawing conclusions.
